text: The video shows the herniated transverse colon within the Morgagni hernia. The surgical port placement is also displayed.

The surgeon uses a ten millimeter supraumbilical port for the camera and two working ports in the left quadrant to avoid adhesions from a previous right subcostal incision. The herniated transverse colon is visible within the Morgagni hernia, with the hepatic flexure adhesed to the right upper quadrant abdominal wall. The hernia contents are reduced, and the hernia sac attachments are divided.
The surgeon dissects and removes as much of the falciform ligament as possible, revealing the thin mesothelial lining of the hernia cavity, which is part of the hernia sac.

The surgeon begins closing the Morgagni hernia defect by taking a bite at the mouth of the defect. A suture passer is introduced into the abdomen to grasp the suture, which is then brought out through the anterior abdominal wall. An Endo Stitch is also brought out, and pledgets are applied. The surgeon takes a second bite using a horizontal mattress technique, cuts the needle end, and uses the suture passer to pull the suture end out onto the abdominal wall, engaging the pledget with the tissue. This process is repeated until all sutures are applied, and the transabdominal sutures are tied down to close the hernia defect.

By taking down as much of the falciform ligament to reduce downward traction, we were able to achieve a tension-free primary repair. The patient was observed overnight and discharged home the next morning. On one week follow-up, he was doing very well and was not taking any pain medications.
